title: AI Radar
subtitle: Anand's point of view on LLM / Generative AI tools, techniques, platforms, and frameworks
description: This radar captures Anand's current point of view on LLM and generative AI tools, techniques, platforms, and frameworks. Each node is evidence-backed with source tags, an importance score (1-10), a confidence score (1-10), and history when the stance appears to have evolved.

techniques.label: Techniques
techniques.description: Prompting, verification, orchestration, workflow patterns, and ways of working with AI.

tools.label: Tools
tools.description: Specific CLIs, coding agents, browsers, and operator tools used directly.

platforms.label: Platforms
platforms.description: Access models, infrastructure choices, long-lived context stores, and broader surfaces AI runs on.

frameworks.label: Frameworks & Protocols
frameworks.description: Conventions, integration patterns, protocols, schemas, and reusable documentation structures for AI-native systems.

adopt.label: Adopt
adopt.description: Strong default. Repeatedly recommended, personally used, and backed by consistent evidence.

trial.label: Trial
trial.description: Useful in bounded settings. Worth active pilots or selective use, but not yet the universal default.

assess.label: Assess
assess.description: Interesting and directionally promising, but still forming or not yet backed by enough repeated practice.

hold.label: Hold
hold.description: Explicit caution. Avoid as a default for new work unless there is a very specific reason.

nodes.name: AI As Interns, Not Search Engines
nodes.description: Use models and agents like capable interns, not like search engines. Give them a bigger goal, enough context to work with, and room to take the first pass, then review what they give you. This works best when you want draft work, validation, or help thinking through a task, but it still needs human checking at the end.

source_tags.search_text: You've hired an intern
Don't treat it like a search engine.
Treat AI like an intern that handles 80-90% of the effort

nodes.name: Multi-Model Double-Checking
nodes.description: A safe default is to have AI check AI. Ask one model to find mistakes, add citations or tests where possible, and then use another model to cross-check the result. If they agree, you have more confidence; if they disagree, that is the point where a human should step in and review manually.

source_tags.search_text: Double-checking with another LLM,
**Have AI cross-check AI**.
Quintuple-check

history.note: Generalized from one tactic into a standard trust pattern for personal use, coding, and organizational adoption.

source_tags.search_text: **Have AI cross-check AI**.
Quintuple-check

history.note: Empirical results made the approach credible: multiple cheap models reduced shared error sharply enough to justify human review only on disagreements.

source_tags.search_text: Double-checking with another LLM,
With 4 LLMs, it was only 1%.

nodes.name: Directional Feedback
nodes.description: When you are not the subject-matter expert, give direction about taste, audience, and the feeling you want instead of pretending to know the implementation. Say how the result should feel, how you will know it is right, and what problem it should solve. This gives the agent room to use its judgment, which helps most when the goal is partly subjective.

source_tags.search_text: **Directional feedback**.
There's a different approach: **directional feedback**.
Trust the agent.

nodes.name: Prototype the Prototype
nodes.description: If the idea is still fuzzy, ask the agent to build a rough first version instead of spending time debating the perfect one. A draft is easier to inspect, change, or throw away than a long list of possible ideas. This is useful when speed matters and you learn more by seeing something real.

source_tags.search_text: **Prototype the prototype**.
**Build, don't plan**.
Build them all. Throw away what doesn't work.

nodes.name: Context Engineering With Local Corpora
nodes.description: Give agents broad access to the local material that already matters: notes, transcripts, repos, prompts, and other records you have organized. That stored context becomes part of the work, not just background noise. It helps most when the answer depends on your own history, but it only works well if the material is kept usable and current.

source_tags.search_text: **Organize context**.
managed [digital exhaust](/blog/digital-exhaust/) is an asset.
This is not a new technique. It's just context engineering

history.note: Moved from general context-collection into an explicit operating model with read-only local mounts, scan instructions, sub-agents, and JSON outputs.

source_tags.search_text: STEP 1: Give access to content
STEP 2: Ask agents to scan content
STEP 4: Output as JSON

history.note: The value of managed personal context was already explicit, but mostly framed as digital exhaust and organized inputs for agents.

source_tags.search_text: **Mine your digital exhaust**.
**Organize context**.

nodes.name: Use Code for Analysis and Deterministic Checks
nodes.description: When accuracy matters, ask the model to write code, run checks, or work in a deterministic setup instead of relying on raw prose reasoning. Code is easier to verify and usually fails in more obvious ways than a free-form answer. This is especially helpful for numbers, analysis, and tasks where you can test the result directly.

source_tags.search_text: **Have LLMs write code**.
**Have it write code to process numbers**.
**Ask for code** to generate the answer - instead of the answer.

nodes.name: AI-Native Assessment and Execution Logs
nodes.description: If bots can already solve the old task, change what you measure. Focus on the workflow, the tools, the debugging steps, the recovery path, and the execution trace, not just the final polished output. That matters anywhere you want to know whether someone can actually run the process, not just produce an answer that looks good.

source_tags.search_text: Learning lives in the **execution logs**.
Use repo-grounded vivas for authenticity
Grade students on designing AI workflows

history.note: Expanded from the problem statement into a more complete assessment stack: repo-grounded vivas, process logs, workflow design, and hands-on AI tasks.

source_tags.search_text: Make process logs part of the evidence
Use repo-grounded vivas for authenticity
Grade students on designing AI workflows

history.note: The old exam model was already being questioned once coding agents could solve the task quickly and in parallel.

source_tags.search_text: If a bot can pass my exam, what exactly am I teaching?
Learning lives in the **execution logs**.

nodes.name: Vibe Coding for Throwaway or Internal Work
nodes.description: Use vibe coding when speed matters more than certainty, especially for prototypes, personal tools, internal workflows, and early tests of an idea. It can unblock work that would otherwise stay stuck in planning. It fits best when failure is cheap and the code does not need to be durable right away.

source_tags.search_text: Vibe coding can accelerate throw-away prototypes.
**Vibe code first**.
Start with new projects: less competition, fewer preconceptions, lower risk.

nodes.name: Production Vibe Coding Without Review
nodes.description: Do not treat fast vibe-coded work as production-ready by default. Unreviewed code can pile up technical debt quickly, and fixing it later can take far longer than expected. For durable software, you need stronger rules, tests, logs, and human accountability before you trust it.

source_tags.search_text: This added technical debt.
Vibe-coding is for unproduced, not production, code
If it works, AND is what you want, AND needs to be maintainable, THEN look at code.

history.note: The boundary became explicit: vibe coding is excellent for dormant ideas and prototypes, but not a free pass to ship production code unchanged.

source_tags.search_text: Vibe coding can accelerate throw-away prototypes.
not production, code

history.note: Early hands-on use already showed leverage, but also inconsistency, missed edge cases, and expensive cleanup.

source_tags.search_text: This added technical debt.
Codex saved me 90% effort.

nodes.name: Explicit Reasoning Budgets
nodes.description: Reasoning effort is a real setting, not a small detail to ignore. More reasoning can make smaller models much better at judging or verifying work, but it also costs more and takes longer. Use that extra effort on the cases that need it, and keep the cost budget explicit instead of relying on defaults.

source_tags.search_text: Reasoning can make smaller models **much** more powerful.
beware default settings
Use reasoning models only for the borderline cases

history.note: Reasoning moved from a model-quality discovery into an operating rule: cheap screening first, expensive reasoning only for borderline or high-stakes cases.

source_tags.search_text: Use reasoning models only for the borderline cases

history.note: The importance of explicit reasoning settings became obvious when default minimal reasoning broke an evaluator and medium reasoning fixed it.

source_tags.search_text: reasoning_effort
Reasoning can make smaller models **much** more powerful.

nodes.name: Audio-First AI Use
nodes.description: Speaking and listening gives AI more context than typing alone, and it keeps you more present. In practice, voice works well when you want to think out loud, talk while walking, or dump a lot of context quickly. It is less useful when you need careful editing or quiet precision, but it can be a strong way to use dead time and turn rough thoughts into something structured.

source_tags.search_text: **Use audio**.
Use voice mode on mobile
Voice is a Powerful Modality

nodes.name: Supervisory Control Patterns
nodes.description: Using AI well is mostly a supervision job, not a trust problem. Check it against an answer sheet, a checklist, samples, and other responses, then ask whether the result actually worked in the real world. These patterns matter because they catch errors without needing perfect autonomy, and they work best when you can define clear criteria or review a meaningful sample.

source_tags.search_text: **Verify**. Check against an "answer sheet".
**Checklist**. Evaluate against pre-defined criteria.
**Outcome**. Did it work in the real world?

nodes.name: Codex CLI
nodes.description: Codex CLI is useful because it acts like a working tool, not just a chat window. It can write code while you walk, drive browser-based tasks, and run small automations that update themselves as edge cases show up. That makes it practical for hands-on work, especially when the task is clear enough to hand off but still needs code that can be reused.

source_tags.search_text: I use [Codex](https://chatgpt.com/codex) to write tools while I walk.
I solved one question with [Codex CLI](https://developers.openai.com/codex/cli/).
I use Codex to classify my expense receipts.

history.note: Codex moved from a code-generation helper into an execution wrapper around reusable software and data-processing tasks.

source_tags.search_text: I use Codex to classify my expense receipts.

history.note: Early use already saved large amounts of effort, but also surfaced technical debt and the need for stronger constraints.

source_tags.search_text: Codex saved me 90% effort.
This added technical debt.

nodes.name: Claude Code
nodes.description: Claude Code is a strong coding-agent tool for big outputs, UI work, and one-shot repo tasks. It is useful when you want to run with prior context instead of building a full reusable system first. But it can still hit output limits or session issues, so it works best when you keep an eye on cost, context size, and the exact model behavior you need.

source_tags.search_text: I just ran Claude with prior context.
Claude Code supports [32K output tokens]
Prefer Codex > Claude Code.

nodes.name: Playwright + CDP + Rodney
nodes.description: Browser automation is part of a normal AI workflow here. Playwright, CDP on localhost:9222, and Rodney-style automation help verify frontend work, inspect the page directly, and solve real browser tasks instead of guessing from screenshots. This matters most when UI details are easy to miss by eye, and it is less useful if you only need a quick visual check.

source_tags.search_text: **Use Playwright to verify**.
Use CDP at localhost:9222.
Use CDP to visit https://exam.sanand.workers.dev/tds-2025-09-roe and solve Q4.

nodes.name: llm CLI
nodes.description: A general LLM CLI belongs in the daily toolkit because it fits many small jobs. It can handle transcription, OCR, quick prompts, vision inputs, embeddings, and shell pipelines without forcing you into a chat app. That makes it especially handy for scripts and automation, while still being simple enough for ad hoc use when you just want to ask a model something fast.

nodes.name: Google Workspace CLI
nodes.description: A unified Google Workspace CLI is appealing because it gives agents one headless entry point for a large set of authenticated tools. Instead of wiring up separate custom integrations for each service, the agent can use a single interface that is easier to script and reason about. It works best when you want broad access across Workspace APIs, and less well if you need something outside that shared surface.

source_tags.search_text: Google Workspace CLI
single unified service for all Google Workspace APIs
gws (Google Workspace CLI), gcloud, aws

nodes.name: Sub-Agents and Parallel Agent Runs
nodes.description: Sub-agents help when work can be split into parallel pieces or when the main context is getting too crowded. They let different threads explore independently and can speed up scanning large sets of content. The tradeoff is real, though: they use more tokens and need clear delegation, or they can create duplicate work and extra noise.

source_tags.search_text: When possible, run in parallel: read, tools, sub-agents, ...
Use sub-agents as required
Using sub-agents spends ~15x more tokens.

history.note: Sub-agents became a practical corpus-scanning tactic, especially when combined with local context repositories and explicit output schemas.

source_tags.search_text: Use sub-agents as required

history.note: The accuracy gains were interesting, but the token cost and orchestration burden were already obvious.

source_tags.search_text: Using sub-agents spends ~15x more tokens.
Teach the orchestrator how to delegate

nodes.name: Paid Frontier AI Subscriptions
nodes.description: A paid subscription to a frontier consumer AI product is treated as a baseline, not a luxury. The paid models are much better than the free ones, and the privacy terms are cleaner because your data is not used to train the models. In practice, this is a small monthly cost compared with the quality gain, especially if you use AI often enough to notice the difference.

source_tags.search_text: **Buy a paid AI subscription of [ChatGPT](https://chatgpt.com/pricing), [Claude](https://claude.com/pricing), or [Gemini](https://gemini.google/subscriptions/)**
This is the best $20/month you can spend.
**Use the best models**.

nodes.name: Shared Governed Enterprise AI Access
nodes.description: Organizations should offer shared, budgeted, governed access to approved AI models instead of leaving it to personal subscriptions or shadow IT. This keeps AI as a normal organizational capability, with legal cover and clearer privacy boundaries, rather than a private advantage for whoever pays out of pocket. It works best when the company already has whitelisted enterprise tenants and still keeps humans responsible for the final judgment.

source_tags.search_text: Every company has its own white-listed enterprise models
Give every student shared, budgeted AI access
Use this. It provides legal cover and data won't be used for training.

nodes.name: API-First Model Consumption
nodes.description: For ordinary work, APIs are usually a better choice than running your own frontier models. They tend to cost less, stay fresher, and drop more easily into orchestration layers, workflows, and structured output pipelines. In practice, that means less time spent on model ops and more time building the actual tool or workflow.

source_tags.search_text: APIs are cheaper than self hosting
**APIs are cheaper than self hosting.** Avoid self-hosting.
Build orchestration layers and proprietary data workflows instead.

nodes.name: Self-Hosting Frontier Models for Normal Work
nodes.description: Do not make self-hosting your default for routine use. The setup and upkeep cost more, and frontier models improve so fast that a local copy can age badly before it pays off. It can make sense in special cases, but for normal work managed APIs are the safer bet.

source_tags.search_text: Don't bother running your own models.
**APIs are cheaper than self hosting.** Avoid self-hosting.
**Buy, don't build**. Don't train models.

nodes.name: Agentic Dev Container Workstation
nodes.description: A dev container built for agents should be the standard workstation, not an afterthought. The point is to give the agent a controlled place where it can code, use the browser, and run tools repeatedly without making a mess of the host system. That matters because the environment shapes what the agent can do safely and reliably, even more than the model itself.

source_tags.search_text: I use a [Dockerfile](https://github.com/sanand0/scripts/blob/7e1dc00d7e1fa36a9949a1e061c1b529928cc175/dev.dockerfile) and [script](https://github.com/sanand0/scripts/blob/7e1dc00d7e1fa36a9949a1e061c1b529928cc175/dev.sh) to isolate coding agents.
Run a coding agent CLI inside the container:
npm install -g @openai/codex@latest;

nodes.name: Personal Data and Browser History as Memory Assets
nodes.description: Treat browsing history, transcripts, notes, logs, and other personal data as memory you can reuse. This is not about saving everything for its own sake; it is about helping an agent remember what happened, spot patterns, and answer with better context later. It works best when the data is organized enough to search and pass along, and it fails when the records are too messy or incomplete to trust.

source_tags.search_text: **Mine your digital exhaust**.
browser history is a _great_ way to "remember" this.
**Organize context**.

history.note: The memory idea broadened from browser history into larger local context repositories that agents can scan directly.

source_tags.search_text: This gives read-only access to my blog, things I learned, transcripts

history.note: Browser history proved itself as a practical memory aid and retrieval surface rather than a novelty.

source_tags.search_text: This is a _fantastic_ way to jog my memory

nodes.name: Agents as the New Software Interface
nodes.description: The direction is that agents will become the main user of software, and sometimes the wrapper around it too. That is already starting to show up in how people think about the web and tools for coding agents. But this is still emerging, so it is more a trend to watch and shape than a settled way of working.

source_tags.search_text: The Internet is forking into a human internet vs an agent web
**Agents are the new users** of software.
Coding agents ARE the new software

history.note: The idea moved from a broad intuition about an agent web into an explicit claim that coding agents are becoming the wrapper around all software.

source_tags.search_text: The Internet is forking into a human internet vs an agent web
I see a future where coding agents are the wrapper around _all_ software.

nodes.name: AGENTS.md + README.md + SKILL.md
nodes.description: Shared instruction files and skill docs are part of how a repo runs, not just extra documentation. They carry intent, style, useful defaults, and past lessons so agents do not need the same explanation over and over. They work best when they stay current and focus on rules that will still make sense as models improve.

nodes.name: Agent-Friendly CLI Design
nodes.description: CLI tools should be built with agents in mind from the start. Structured input and output, schema-like help, dry runs, validation, and compact docs make them easier for both humans and agents to use without guesswork. This matters most for repeatable tasks and stateless tools, where clear machine-readable behavior saves time and reduces mistakes.

source_tags.search_text: agent-friendly CLI practices
Support JSON input via `--json` or `--params`
Add a schema or `--describe` capability

nodes.name: Direct CLI Tooling Over APIs for Developers
nodes.description: For developers, a direct CLI is often easier for an agent to use than a raw API. Smaller models tend to handle shell commands more reliably, and the operator can lean on familiar docs, completions, and command-line habits. This works best for simple stateless tools; it is less ideal when the integration is complex or needs richer state.

source_tags.search_text: When using AI coding agents, CLI beats APIs.
Prefer CLIs for stateless tools, MCPs for stateful, authenticated, or complex
fd, find

nodes.name: MCP for Stateful or Authenticated Integrations
nodes.description: MCP is useful when you need to package a tool for other teams, non-developers, or clients, or when the integration is stateful, authenticated, or awkward to flatten into a simple CLI. For many developer workflows, plain tool calling is still enough. It shines when the wrapper matters, but it is not the default answer for every case.

source_tags.search_text: MCPs aren't much more useful than tool calling _for developers_.
They're powerful when packaging for external parties
Prefer CLIs for stateless tools, MCPs for stateful, authenticated, or complex

history.note: The stance became more precise: use CLIs for stateless tools and reserve MCP for stateful, authenticated, or complex integrations.

nodes.name: llms.txt and Published Skills Docs
nodes.description: Machine-readable docs like `llms.txt` and published skill directories look promising because they can make agent context cheaper and easier to move around. They give models a cleaner way to find the right instructions instead of digging through large sites or long pages. The idea is useful, but the ecosystem is still settling, so the patterns are not fully standardized yet.

history.note: The concept expanded from `llms.txt` into published skills directories and broader LLM-facing documentation surfaces.

nodes.name: Evals, Binary Rubrics, and LLM-as-a-Judge
nodes.description: Evaluation should be a normal part of AI work, not an afterthought. In practice, that means breaking fuzzy tasks into yes/no checks, asking the model to explain itself before it judges, and adding review tiers for higher-risk cases. This matters because it makes model behavior easier to compare, spot-check, and trust when results start to drift.

source_tags.search_text: **Standardize evaluation**.
Break rubrics into binary sub-criteria; reason before judging
Govern with green/amber/red review levels

history.note: Evaluation matured from model cross-checking into a full audit pattern with binary rubrics, shadow mode, executable policy, and reasoning reserved for edge cases.

source_tags.search_text: Break rubrics into binary sub-criteria; reason before judging

nodes.name: Open Responses API
nodes.description: An open Responses-style layer matters because it could make higher-level agent tooling easier to move across providers. That can reduce lock-in if the same interface works across more of the stack. It is still something to watch rather than something to build around today, because broad support is not there yet.

source_tags.search_text: [Open Responses](https://www.openresponses.org/) is the open version of OpenAI's Responses API.

nodes.name: Custom Model Training as Strategy
nodes.description: Custom model training should not be the center of the strategy. The safer move is to keep your data, prompts, orchestration, and workflows, while letting model vendors keep racing on the base models. This works best when you already have useful datasets and want the option to fine-tune later, but it is a bad bet if you expect the custom model itself to stay ahead for long.

source_tags.search_text: Building custom models does not.
**Buy, don't build**. Don't train models.
**Datasets matter**.

history.note: The position hardened into explicit organizational advice: buy the models, keep the orchestration and data workflows, and avoid anchoring strategy on training your own model.

source_tags.search_text: **Buy, don't build**. Don't train models.

history.note: The earlier framing already downgraded custom models and elevated proprietary datasets instead.

source_tags.search_text: **Datasets matter**.

It also gives a clearer choice between Fast, Thinking, and Pro instead of forcing one model to handle every job the same way. It works well for broad, mixed tasks, but like any model it still has limits in some media workflows.

nodes.name: Gemini Deep Research as Cited Brief Builder
nodes.description: Gemini Deep Research is useful when you need a cited brief from many sources, not just a quick answer. In practice, it can gather enough material for a client memo, outreach draft, or slide outline, and it can even use private files where available. It is strongest when you need a careful summary with references, and weaker when you need a fast one-shot reply.

source_tags.search_text: - 🟢 [Gemini Deep Research](https://gemini.google/overview/deep-research/) is Gemini's agentic research mode that browses many sources (optionally your Gmail/Drive/Chat) and produces multi-page reports. Use it to build a _client brief with citations_ (market, competitors, risks), then reuse it for outreach or a deck outline.
Gemini Deep Research accepts files and images. Cross-checking reports, providing private sources, etc. is now realistic.
It's interesting that Gemini Deep Research cannot access Google Drive while Gemini can.

history.note: The feature became interesting once it could accept files and images and cross-check private sources instead of only browsing public web pages.

nodes.name: Gemini Canvas for Research-to-Artifact Conversion
nodes.description: Gemini Canvas helps turn research into something people can actually share and use. Instead of leaving a report as a long document, you can shape it into a page, quiz, or simple app that works better for internal sharing or explanation. It is especially handy when the goal is to make the output easier to browse, not just easier to read.

source_tags.search_text: - 🟢 [Gemini Canvas](https://gemini.google/overview/canvas/) turns ideas (and Deep Research reports) into shareable artifacts like web pages, quizzes, and simple apps. Use it to convert a research report into an _interactive explainer page_ your team can share internally.

nodes.name: Google Workspace with Gemini

nodes.name: Model Cost as Labor Economics
nodes.description: Think about model choice in labor terms: how much work does it cost per hour, and what kind of wage does that map to? That framing helps show when automation becomes cheap enough to matter, and when a model is basically competing with a human worker. It is especially useful for comparing different runs and for judging whether a task is worth automating at all.

source_tags.search_text: If we multiple the Cost Per Output Token with Tokens Per Second, we can get the cost for what an LLM produces in Dollars Per Hour. (We’re ignoring the input cost, but it’s not the main driver of time.)
Once a language model’s run-time cost drops below the local minimum wage, the “offshoring” advantage disappears.

- **It makes curiosity cheaper**. I wouldn't have sat and written the scripts to figure out where $6.71 went. But coding agents made micro-audits practical (and with clever use of `jaq`, `csvq`, and `duckdb`. If you can get an answer by just from a question, we'd use it like Google - to **answer ad hoc questions**.
The cost of intelligence is trending to zero. How do we plan for this?

history.note: The first framing was raw API-versus-GPU economics, where the surprising result was that APIs were dramatically cheaper than self-hosting.

source_tags.search_text: So, the API is between 60 - 700 **times** cheaper than running it yourself. 60 times cheaper if you move from Lambda Labs to Azure AI Foundry. 700 times cheaper if you move from Azure servers to OpenRouter.

history.note: I then reframed model cost as dollars per hour and compared it to minimum wage, which made the labor economics argument concrete.

source_tags.search_text: If we multiple the Cost Per Output Token with Tokens Per Second, we can get the cost for what an LLM produces in Dollars Per Hour. (We’re ignoring the input cost, but it’s not the main driver of time.)
The latest Claude 4 Sonnet costs ~$2/hr -- India's minimum wage rate.

nodes.name: Browser Workspaces as a Focus/Memory Surface
nodes.description: Use browser workspaces to group active browsing around real contexts like work, reading, chores, or AI instead of leaving everything in one tab pile. That makes it easier to stay oriented across devices and to treat browser history as a kind of memory for what you were doing. It works best when your browsing has recurring themes, and less well if your work is too scattered to cluster.

source_tags.search_text: Every day, I interact with a barrage of websites: emails, news, social media, and work tools across multiple devices. [Microsoft Edge’s workspaces](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/deployedge/microsoft-edge-workspaces) syncs groups of websites across devices. I've never tried it, started today, and wondered: **how should I organize my workspaces?**
> Here are the sites I visit, with rough frequency. On Microsoft Edge, I can create workspaces. Based on this browsing behavior, what kinds of workspaces might I create? Give me multiple options.
The main gap now is when I'm at home, not spending money or meeting people. Just... browsing. And browser history is a _great_ way to "remember" this.

That matters most when the output has legal, contractual, or safety consequences.

nodes.name: Emotion and Persona Prompt Hacks
nodes.description: Most emotional, persuasive, or persona-flavored prompt rewrites do not buy much. If prompt wording helps at all, simple reasoning cues like step-by-step thinking are the ones that show a small benefit, and even that is limited. The effect is uneven across models and tasks, so these hacks are more of a weak nudge than a reliable method.

source_tags.search_text: - **"Think step by step" (Reasoning)** is the only prompt variant that _very slightly_ improves overall accuracy across the 40 models tested, and even that edge is modest (+3.5 percentage-points vs the model's own _Normal_ wording, _p ~ 0.06_).
- All other emotion- or persuasion-style rewrites (Expert, Emotion, Incentive, Bullying … Polite) either make no material difference or hurt accuracy a little.
- Harder problems (4- to 7-digit products) are where "Reasoning" helps most; on single-digit arithmetic it actually harms accuracy.

This is especially important in real deployments, where the surrounding system has to handle mistakes, not just produce answers.

replace “vibe testing” with repeatable scoring

nodes.name: Fact-Check Flagging Instead of Final Verdicts
nodes.description: Use AI to point out likely mistakes and suspicious claims, then let a person decide. This is useful because the model does not need to be perfectly reliable to be helpful; it just needs to surface things worth checking. It works well for finding weak spots, but the final truth call still belongs to the reviewer.

source_tags.search_text: Using LLMs to find errors is fairly hallucination-proof.
flag questionable claims for [further review]
**Validation**. AI works fast. Learn shortcuts to compare versions, find mistakes, give feedback

nodes.name: Public Inspectability for AI Governance
nodes.description: Make AI systems, courses, and policies open enough that others can inspect how they work. Openness makes criticism, reuse, and improvement easier, and it helps keep governance from depending on one person’s private judgment. It also works as a check on secret prompts and closed procedures that are hard to trust.

source_tags.search_text: [**Make the course publicly inspectable**]
Third, it's **easier to audit**.
**Secret prompts don't help**.

nodes.name: Provenance and Watermark Checks Before Release
nodes.description: Before anything goes out, check whether the content can be identified as AI-made or AI-edited. Watermarking and provenance checks help route risky items for review and make it easier to handle regulated or sensitive content. This matters most when an organization publishes a lot and needs a way to tell what came from its tools.

source_tags.search_text: provenance checks before external release
watermark outbound “AI-assisted” customer emails and automatically route them for review if they’re about regulated topics

nodes.name: Human Sign-Off as Insurance
nodes.description: When AI makes output cheap, human sign-off becomes more like reassurance than raw labor. People are often paying for someone to stand behind the result, carry the reputational risk, or make the decision feel safer. That matters most when trust is the product and less when the task is simple production.

source_tags.search_text: **You're buying insurance**.
**Companies sell procedure**.
Long-term retainers reserved for trust.

nodes.name: Liability-Backed AI Accountability
nodes.description: Treat accountability as something that needs real teeth, not just good intentions. Liability, insurance, locked capital, and formal rules give victims a place to recover losses and give operators something to lose if they cause harm. This approach matters most for economically important systems, where promises without sanctions are too weak to matter.

source_tags.search_text: If we need to, AI can be held to account.
Agents must maintain locked capital or insurance.
liability regimes that internalize tail risk

nodes.name: Layered Guardrails and Policy Tests
nodes.description: Protect AI systems with layers, not a single gate. Input filters, output moderation, policy tests, and subgroup checks catch different failure modes, so one missed case does not turn into a bigger incident. This works best when you test the system from several angles, because jailbreaks, safety bugs, and subgroup failures do not all look the same.

source_tags.search_text: layered defenses (input filtering + output moderation + policy tests)
automatically flag accuracy issues, safety risks, and PII leaks.
slice analysis + counterfactual edits + feature sensitivity

nodes.name: Speak-to-Build Workflows
nodes.description: Use voice when speaking keeps the work moving better than typing or switching windows. It is a good fit for drafting slides, coding, or interviewing yourself while walking, because the tool stays in the flow instead of breaking it. The approach works less well when you need quiet precision or when the setup makes it hard to listen and respond smoothly.

nodes.name: Realtime Voice Bot Architecture
nodes.description: Build live voice systems around one real-time audio loop instead of separate speech-to-text and text-to-speech calls. That setup is better for low-latency, human-like back-and-forth, especially when the bot needs to know when it is listening and when it should answer. It also depends on practical details like a good microphone and enough context to recognize names and other tricky words.

source_tags.search_text: specifically designed for low-latency, human-like voice interactions.
which should be replaced by a single real-time audio loop.

nodes.name: Local Accent Fidelity Over Perfect Audio
nodes.description: For multilingual voice products, getting the local accent and pronunciation right matters more than making the audio sound flawless. A polished voice with the wrong regional tone can break trust fast, especially in professional settings. In practice, this works best when a human checks tricky words and domain terms, because AI can still miss the nuance.

source_tags.search_text: Prioritize cultural tone and local dialect over pure technical audio quality, as a "perfect" voice with the wrong regional accent can ruin professional credibility.

nodes.name: Document-to-Audio Overview Platforms
nodes.description: NotebookLM-style platforms can turn documents, archives, or even commit history into narrated overviews and scripts. That makes long or dense text easier to absorb, especially when people would rather listen than read. They work well for rough knowledge transfer and summaries, but they still depend on good source material and careful shaping of the final narrative.

nodes.name: One Source, Many Modalities
nodes.description: Treat a single talk, video, or document as a source for many different outputs instead of rewriting everything from scratch each time. The same material can become slides, articles, storybooks, infographics, or audio, which makes information easier to reuse and reach different audiences. This works especially well when the goal is speed and reach, but it still needs editing so each format fits its job.

source_tags.search_text: But it's also fascinating to see how much SECONDARY content you can generate from a video.

The way in which we consume information is entirely up to us. nodes.name: Slideshow-as-Video AI Pipeline
nodes.description: Build AI video as a sequence of small slide-like parts, each with its own script, still image, and audio. That way, you can fix one section without regenerating the whole video. It is a practical fit for explainer content and early prototypes, but it is less useful when you need highly fluid or cinematic motion.

nodes.name: Tier-1 Raw Materials Handoff
nodes.description: For video work, the most useful AI output is often not a finished video, but a tidy folder of raw parts the team can finish quickly. That usually means a script, voiceover, slide plan, and a set of candidate images arranged in a clear structure. It helps because humans can polish the last mile, but it will not replace review or assembly.

source_tags.file: Dropbox/notes/transcripts/extract/ai/2026-03-03 WK Wolters Kluwer Verdict to Video.md
source_tags.search_text: *   **The "Raw Materials" handoff:** The most effective AI output for video creation is a structured "Tier 1" folder containing the script, a voiceover file, and candidate images organized by slide for a human to finalize.
*   **Show visual mockups of AI deliverables**, such as folder structures, to prevent the team from assuming the AI is producing a different final format like a PowerPoint.
*   **Use LLMs to auto-extract metadata** from large asset libraries to make thousands of images searchable via text-based prompts.

nodes.name: Character Cards and Reference Images for Consistency
nodes.description: Use reference images and explicit character cards when you need the same person or visual identity to stay stable across many generated images. This helps with slides, storybooks, and comics where consistency matters more than one perfect frame. It works well for repeatable characters and expressions, but it still needs human review because the model can drift or make mistakes.

nodes.name: Style Transfer as Creative Accelerator
nodes.description: Ask AI to rewrite in the style of a strong writer, publication, or creator when you want quality to improve quickly. You do not always need to spell out every detail if the style gives the model a clear target. This is useful for making drafts more engaging, but it still works best when you compare several versions and choose what fits.

source_tags.search_text: **Style transfer** is my newly discovered AI super-power: having AI rewrite in someone's style.

At least for the next few years, the ROI is less from expertise. It's more from _style_.

nodes.name: Style Libraries as Prompt Infrastructure
nodes.description: Keep a reusable library of style descriptors and prompt fragments so new creative work can start from known options instead of a blank page. A good gallery makes it easy to pick from a growing set of styles and reuse them across projects. It works best when the team wants speed and consistency, but the library still needs upkeep as tastes and needs change.

nodes.name: Comics as Explanation Interface
nodes.description: Use AI-generated comics as a teaching surface when you want an idea to feel more concrete and memorable. Characters, speech bubbles, and simple scenes can explain a concept in a way that is easier to scan than plain text. This works especially well for lessons, quizzes, and infographics, but it is less useful when the topic needs deep detail or formal tone.

nodes.name: Two-Stage Prompting for Image Generation
nodes.description: Use one step or one model to come up with the creative brief, then a second step or model to render the image from that brief. This split helps because the model that invents ideas is not always the same one that follows instructions best. It is useful when prompts need help getting sharper, but it still leaves room for small visual errors that humans can spot.

nodes.name: Abstract / Comic Styles to Dodge the Uncanny Valley
nodes.description: When realism is hard to fake well, abstract styles like comics, sketch notes, or infographics are a safer path. They are easier for AI to produce and usually draw less criticism than lifelike images. In practice, this works best when the goal is to explain an idea clearly, not to imitate reality exactly.

nodes.name: AI-Guided Perceptual Media Compression
nodes.description: This is about using AI to compare many compression settings and help you decide with your own eyes and ears. Instead of trusting defaults, you can ask for test variations, side-by-side checks, and questions that make your preferences clearer. It works best when small quality changes matter, like color shifts or dropped frames, and less well when the output is too subjective to judge quickly.

nodes.name: Cross-Language Script Mining with Multimodal AI
nodes.description: This node is about taking audio or documents in a language you do not speak and turning them into usable scripts or explainers. AI can transcribe, translate, and reshape the material so you can work with it directly instead of getting stuck at the language barrier. It is especially useful for source-heavy work, but it still needs review when the meaning is subtle or the source is messy.

nodes.name: AI as Generator, Not Micro-Editor for Visual Layouts
nodes.description: The point here is to use AI for the big first pass, not for tiny visual tweaks. It can generate draft assets and rough layouts well enough, but it tends to break when you ask for small nudges or exact spatial changes. That makes it useful for building from scratch or filling in pieces, and much less reliable for fine layout work that depends on precise control.

source_tags.file: Dropbox/notes/transcripts/extract/ai/2026-03-03 WK Wolters Kluwer Verdict to Video.md
source_tags.search_text: *   **Treat AI as a generator, not an editor:** AI should be viewed as a powerful but blunt generator rather than an iterative design tool for fine-tuning micro-details or specific visual layouts.

source_tags.file: Dropbox/notes/transcripts/extract/ai/2026-03-03 WK Wolters Kluwer Verdict to Video.md
source_tags.search_text: *   **The "Nudge" trap:** Do not use AI as an iterative slide editor, as asking it to make minor adjustments often causes it to "break" the other 75% of the slide that was previously correct.

source_tags.search_text: *   **Be wary of spatial reasoning limits:** Current LLMs have architectural limitations in spatial reasoning and multimodal layout, making 100% automation without strict templates unrealistic in the short term.
AI image generation still has a _long_ way to go.

nodes.name: Lineage-First Analytical Outputs
nodes.description: Analytical outputs should show where the answer came from, not just the final conclusion. That means keeping a clear trail back to the source data, using validation reports, and generating code that people can inspect or rerun. This matters most when the work may be audited or challenged, because it gives humans a way to check the logic instead of trusting the model blindly.

source_tags.search_text: > Finally, double-check the architecture against the codebase and provide a step-by-step validation report.
- Maintain strict data lineage by recording the specific source document, page, and cell for every single entry in the facts table.
- Design the agent to generate verifiable code (such as SQL or Python) to query data rather than allowing it to manipulate numbers directly.

nodes.name: Answer-Only Dataviz Instead of Excel
nodes.description: This is about getting charts and quick analysis directly from a reasoning model instead of opening Excel or a BI tool. It can save time when you just want the answer and a usable visual, especially for small or medium tasks that would otherwise need manual setup. It works less well when the analysis needs heavy spreadsheet work, careful formatting, or a long back-and-forth with the data.

source_tags.search_text: This is a popular visualization, but I wanted to see if I could get the newer OpenAI models to create the visual without me 𝗿𝘂𝗻𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 any code (i.e. I just want the answer.) After a couple of iterations, O3 did a great job with this prompt:
[O3/O4 Mini](https://openai.com/index/introducing-o3-and-o4-mini/) are starting to replace Excel (or Tableau/Power BI) for quick analysis and visualizations. At least for me.
That's it! I never even looked at the code. The whole loop took 3 minutes - far faster than I could manage, though I'm good at code **and** data visualization!

nodes.name: Public + Private Data Fusion for Bespoke Research
nodes.description: The value here comes from mixing public research with private material like emails, call recordings, transcripts, and delivery examples. That blend helps produce briefs, pitches, and client responses that feel specific instead of generic. It works best when there is a lot of internal knowledge to draw from, and it falls short if the private side is thin or poorly organized.

source_tags.search_text: - **Mining "Tribal Knowledge"**: AI’s unique value lies in its ability to mine internal data—such as email exchanges, call recordings, and transcripts—to synthesize insights that are too voluminous for humans to process.
- **Synthesizing Public and Private Data**: The most effective research comes from using AI to blend external market research with internal "bespoke" data to create highly personalized pitches.
- **Internal Search:** Deploy a coding agent (such as Codex) to search through internal repositories like Google Drive to automatically retrieve relevant project delivery examples and evidence.
- Incorporate meeting transcripts as a primary reference source to provide more context and depth for analyst responses.

nodes.name: Analysis-vs-RAG Query Routing
nodes.description: This pattern starts by deciding what kind of question is being asked before doing any work. A router can send structured questions toward analysis and evidence-seeking questions toward retrieval, or split the job between both. That matters because it keeps the agent from using the wrong method, which is a common reason these systems go off track.

source_tags.search_text: - Use a query router at the start of the process to determine whether a request needs structured data analysis or unstructured retrieval (RAG).
- Design the agent to generate verifiable code (such as SQL or Python) to query data rather than allowing it to manipulate numbers directly.
- **Internal Search:** Deploy a coding agent (such as Codex) to search through internal repositories like Google Drive to automatically retrieve relevant project delivery examples and evidence.

nodes.name: Self-Critique Loops for Domain Analysis
nodes.description: This node is about making the system critique itself before anything is delivered. By comparing its own reasoning with outside feedback and asking it to look for missing evidence, you can catch weak spots earlier. It is useful when quality matters more than speed, but it still needs human judgment because self-critique can miss deeper mistakes.

source_tags.file: Dropbox/notes/transcripts/extract/ai/2025-11-19 Automating research with AI coding agents - workshop.md
source_tags.search_text: - **The Self-Critique Loop**: Using a specific "critique your own analysis" prompt forces the AI to identify its own hallucinations, technical errors, and "financial naivety."

source_tags.file: Dropbox/notes/transcripts/extract/ai/2025-11-19 Automating research with AI coding agents - workshop.md
source_tags.search_text: - **Model Tiering**: Workflows can be optimized by using faster, cheaper "mini" models for initial generation and more sophisticated models (like Gemini or GPT-o1) for the critique phase.
- **Gap Analysis:** Ask the LLM to explain why it gave a specific rating to determine if its reasoning overlaps with real analyst feedback.
- **Iterative Improvement:** Feed both your original response and the analyst’s feedback into the LLM to identify contradictions, missing evidence, and new ideas for improvement.

nodes.name: Domain-Trained Research Agents for Messy Corpora
nodes.description: This is about giving agents the right domain tools and letting them work through messy real-world data instead of forcing a narrow demo case. With access to the right databases, Python libraries, and file structures, they can handle more of the actual research flow. It works best when the task has a known domain shape, and less well when the needed tools or data sources are missing.

source_tags.file: Dropbox/notes/transcripts/extract/ai/2025-11-19 Automating research with AI coding agents - workshop.md
source_tags.search_text: - **Teaching Domain Skills**: AI agents can be "taught" specific domain workflows (like bioinformatics or cheminformatics) by giving them access to specialized databases and Python libraries.

source_tags.file: Dropbox/notes/transcripts/extract/ai/2025-11-19 Automating research with AI coding agents - workshop.md
source_tags.search_text: - **Handling Messy Data**: Modern coding agents are capable of autonomously navigating messy real-world data structures, such as ZIP files with non-standard naming conventions.

source_tags.file: Dropbox/notes/transcripts/extract/ai/2025-11-19 Automating research with AI coding agents - workshop.md
source_tags.search_text: - **Economic Viability**: Deep, multi-step scientific research via agents is now economically feasible, with complex analyses costing as little as $2–$3 per run.
- Improve extraction from complex documents by feeding the LLM multiple formats simultaneously, such as images, raw Excel data, and markdown.

nodes.name: Analytical Micro-Audits and Curiosity-on-Demand
nodes.description: Coding agents can make small audits cheap enough to answer ad hoc questions that you would not normally script by hand. That matters when you want to find hidden cost leaks, test assumptions, or move from asking a question to getting a useful fix. They work best for quick, focused checks and can even suggest changes, but they are still only as good as the question and the data you give them.

source_tags.search_text: - **It makes curiosity cheaper**. I wouldn't have sat and written the scripts to figure out where $6.71 went. But coding agents made micro-audits practical (and with clever use of `jaq`, `csvq`, and `duckdb`. If you can get an answer by just from a question, we'd use it like Google - to **answer ad hoc questions**.

- **It challenges common sense**. I assumed caching was good for speed and compute. But Codex's analysis of my [`sanand0/blog` actions](https://github.com/sanand0/blog/actions) pointed out that dropping a job's time from 1.2 minutes to 0.7 minutes doesn't change the _2-minute billed floor_! Also, the 114MB cache _increased_ storage costs. We can **test optimizations without assuming**.

- **It exposes blind spots**. Turns out that a big chunk of the cost of my (private) [TDS exam](https://exam.sanand.workers.dev/tds-2026-01-ee) repo was `dynamic/copilot-pull-request-reviewer` - a GitHub workflow triggered by using Copilot. I also have a big chunk of "legacy" GitHub Pages on older repos that add to cost because of failures and retries. We can find these invisible leeches draining cost **without knowing what to ask for**.

- **It fixes, not just finds**. Codex rewrote my [blog deployment script](https://github.com/sanand0/blog/commit/2644e9bfcfb4369c2768fbfad599fa939ada2e0f#diff-28802fbf11c83a2eee09623fb192785e7ca92a3f40602a517c011b947a1822d3) to use a single job instead of two, to save compute time. I just copied andd deployed it. It went from **researcher to advisor**. That's a short step from executor, next.

nodes.name: Data-to-Advice Translation with Numerical Heuristics
nodes.description: LLMs can turn cohort data into plain advice instead of a dashboard people have to read themselves. The useful move is to give a short narrative with a few simple numbers, rounded into language people can act on. This works well for teachers and other non-technical users when you want guidance, not a report full of charts.

source_tags.search_text: Can ChatGPT give teachers data-driven **heuristics** on student grades?
> .. give me the best student advice. Phrase it conversationally and include numerical evidence in simple language.
**Teachers**: Try this with **your** course data!
- **Round and contextualize**: "nearly 3 in 4" lands better than "73.2%"; "twice the national average" beats "2.07×".

nodes.name: Reasoning and Agent Loops Have a Ceiling
nodes.description: Reasoning loops help, but only up to a point. Once the problem gets too complex, the model starts to lose the thread, so long-horizon agent loops should not be the default way to solve everything. In practice, this is fine for medium-sized tasks, but it is shaky when you expect an agent to keep going for a long time without help.

source_tags.search_text: - 08 Jun 2025. [The Illusion of Thinking](https://machinelearning.apple.com/research/illusion-of-thinking) paper by Apple shows that reasoning scales only up to a point. Beyond a complexity threshold, models give up. This aligns with [what I saw crudely with mental math](https://sanand0.github.io/llmevals/emotion-prompts/). "Think step by step" helps, but only for medium complexity problems.
Completing long tasks is one measure of intelligence. **Working independently for long** is another. O3 is at ~6 minutes. While it works, I'm practicing Bubble Shooter in 6 minutes!
- Agents (LLMs running tools in a loop) can think only for ~6 min.

history.note: I started treating reasoning as limited by complexity thresholds rather than as a monotonic knob.

source_tags.search_text: reasoning scales only up to a point. Beyond a complexity threshold, models give up.
"Think step by step" helps, but only for medium complexity problems.

history.note: I then measured the time horizon directly and found my longest ChatGPT thinking block was under seven minutes.

source_tags.search_text: How long have _you_ made ChatGPT think? My highest was 6m 50s, with the question:
Completing long tasks is one measure of intelligence. **Working independently for long** is another. O3 is at ~6 minutes. While it works, I'm practicing Bubble Shooter in 6 minutes!

nodes.name: LLM Mental Math Is Still a Hard Boundary
nodes.description: LLMs can sometimes do arithmetic, but they are still not reliable enough to trust with math by default. For anything that needs correct numbers, code or another deterministic check is the safer path. They are better at writing and explaining code than at doing the calculation themselves, which is why running the result matters.

source_tags.search_text: LLMs aren't good tools for math and this is just an informal check.
LLMs are bad at math. They're good at languages, including code. Running the code gives output with low hallucinations.
Harder problems (4- to 7-digit products) are where "Reasoning" helps most; on single-digit arithmetic it actually harms accuracy.

nodes.name: Presentation and Visual Communication Fidelity Remains Fragile
nodes.description: AI can draft slides and other visual artifacts, but the output is still rough and usually needs a human pass. It often misses style rules, struggles with good slide structure, and does not reliably make sketch notes. That makes it useful for first drafts and quick mockups, but not for polished presentation work on its own.

source_tags.search_text: LLMs are still not great at slides.
LLMs still can't follow a data visualization style guide.
LLMs can't yet create good sketch notes.
Gemini can create reasonably good slide drafts that you can export to Google Slides and edit.

nodes.name: Audio Semantics Beyond Transcription Are Weak
nodes.description: These models can transcribe audio, but once you ask them to understand tone, music, or what is happening beyond the words, they often drift into guessing. They can be useful for text from audio, yet they still mis-hear details and may not handle live back-and-forth well. So they work best as transcription tools, not as true listeners.

source_tags.search_text: Gemini can't actually hear the audio. It can transcribe, but beyond that it's just guessing.
Gemini can pass the bar exam and solve maths olympiad puzzles. But it's music-deaf.

nodes.name: Single Recommended Defaults Beat Choice Overload
nodes.description: Most people should get one safe model default instead of being dropped into a long list of choices. That keeps regular users away from choice overload, while still leaving a switch for edge cases like rate limits or a model that cannot handle the task. The default is the main path; manual switching is there as an escape hatch.

source_tags.search_text: Shield the 90% of regular users from "choice overload" by providing a single recommended model default rather than a selection of LLMs.
Applications should include a model selection dropdown so users can manually switch to a different provider if the primary model hits a rate limit.

history.note: The first step was to preserve manual switching so users could escape rate limits or provider issues.

source_tags.search_text: Applications should include a model selection dropdown so users can manually switch to a different provider if the primary model hits a rate limit.

history.note: I later preferred a simpler UX that hides the model menu from most users and presents a single recommended default.

source_tags.search_text: Shield the 90% of regular users from "choice overload" by providing a single recommended model default rather than a selection of LLMs.
Adopt a "provider-native" strategy for AI agents to benefit from free, automatic upgrades and native ecosystem alignment.

nodes.name: Deep Research Connector Gaps Matter
nodes.description: Deep Research is powerful, but it does not always have the same connectors as the normal assistant. That means the evidence it can use may change depending on whether it can reach Drive, chat, files, or other sources. In practice, it is great when the connector you need is there, and less useful when it is missing.

nodes.name: Model-Task Fit Beats Brand Loyalty
nodes.description: The right model depends on the job, not the brand. Some models are better for video, audio, search, or transcription, while others do better at careful instruction following and analysis. In practice, it pays to match the tool to the task and not expect one model to win everywhere.

nodes.name: Markdown + Marp as an LLM-Native Slide Medium
nodes.description: Markdown-first slide making fits the way LLMs already write and revise content, so it lowers friction when you are drafting or editing a deck. Marp then turns that text into slides without forcing you to fight an HTML-first system. It works well for fast, text-heavy presentations and publishing a talk page, but it is less about flashy design than about getting clean slides out quickly.

nodes.name: Gemini-to-Slides Drafting
nodes.description: Gemini can pull in context from across files, shared files, emails, and attachments, so it is useful when a deck needs to start from real material instead of a blank page. In practice, it can make a decent first draft, give you several options, and export to Google Slides for cleanup. That makes it a good fit for fast turnaround work, especially when you want to shape a deck with a client or create something overnight.

nodes.name: Standard Voice with Reasoning Models Over Realtime Voice
nodes.description: For voice work that needs real thought, the better choice is standard voice tied to a stronger reasoning model, not the fastest realtime stack. The lag is a downside, but it can make the system feel like it is actually thinking before it speaks. Realtime voice gives more control over pauses and prompts, yet it can be weaker on model quality or awkward in use, especially when you want careful answers.

source_tags.search_text: 1. [ChatGPT's advanced voice mode](https://help.openai.com/en/articles/8400625-voice-mode-faq): Lets you interrupt it. But if you pause, it replies immediately. Muting caused the app to hang.
2. [Realtime API](https://openai.com/index/introducing-the-realtime-api/): Gave me control of pauses and custom prompts, but used `gpt-4o-realtime-preview` (not as good as `o1`).

3. [Standard voice with `o1`](https://help.openai.com/en/articles/8400625-voice-mode-faq) on [Desktop](https://openai.com/chatgpt/download/): Worked best. It transcribes my speech, sends it to `o1`, and speaks back. There’s a lag, but it feels like it’s thinking.

- **Why not advanced voice mode?** I want to use a reasoning model like [O3 Mini High](https://chatgpt.com/?model=o3-mini-high) for better responses, not the GPT-4o-realtime model that advanced voice mode uses.

history.note: The preference emerges in a slide-building workflow: standard voice is acceptable because it gives access to a stronger reasoning model.

nodes.name: TTS-1 for Literal, Low-Drama Speech
nodes.description: When the price is close, TTS-1 is the safer pick if you want speech that sounds natural and stays close to the text. It does not try to reinterpret what you wrote, so it avoids small changes that can be annoying in things like names, dates, or abbreviations. That makes it a good default for plain, low-drama speech when faithfulness matters more than extra style.

They are less useful when you need final polish or a fully locked-down result.

nodes.description: Image generation is now moving past making new pictures and into being able to follow instructions well enough to replace parts of normal editing software. That matters because it changes the work from manual pixel pushing to asking for edits in plain language. These tools are especially strong for everyday image work, though the big win is in editing and control, not just fresh output.

nodes.name: Relevant Image Generation as an Everyday Publishing Workflow
nodes.description: Generating relevant images has become a normal part of publishing, not a special side task. It shows up in talks, slides, blog posts, and social posts when you need a visual that matches the point you are making. The main value is speed and fit: the image does not have to be perfect, just good enough to support the message and keep the workflow moving.
