nodes.name: Ask AI Buttons in Learning Interfaces
nodes.description: Put an AI entry point right inside the learning interface so students can ask for help where the work is happening. In practice, that means less friction and less pretending that real problem solving is closed-book or done alone. It works best when the goal is support during the task, and it fails if the button is treated like a substitute for thinking.

source_tags.search_text: - ⭐ [**Add an "Ask AI" button**](https://sanand0.github.io/talks/2026-03-18-iitm-academic-council/ideas/table.html?id=ask-ai-button). Make it _easy_ for students to use ChatGPT. Stop pretending that real-world problem solving is closed-book and solo.
In fact, each question now has an "Ask AI" button that dumps the question into your favorite AI tool. Just paste the answer and move on.

nodes.name: Virtual TAs from Real Course Material
nodes.description: Let students build virtual TAs from real course material so the assignment teaches something useful and leaves behind a tool the next class can use. That makes the work feel less disposable, since the output becomes part of the course infrastructure instead of just another submission. It works well when the material is stable enough to reuse, and less well when the course changes too fast for the asset to stay useful.

source_tags.search_text: - [**Let students build virtual TAs from real course material**](https://sanand0.github.io/talks/2026-03-18-iitm-academic-council/ideas/table.html?id=virtual-ta). The project is educational, and the output becomes infrastructure for the next cohort. Good assignments should create assets, not just submissions.

nodes.name: Interactive Explainers for Unfamiliar Concepts
nodes.description: Use AI to turn unfamiliar ideas into visual, animated, or interactive explainers when plain text is too slow or too thin. This helps students and new faculty get oriented faster, especially when they need an intuitive first pass before deeper study. It works best for concept onboarding and weak spots in understanding, but it cannot replace careful teaching when the topic needs precision or depth.

source_tags.search_text: - [**Use interactive explainers for unfamiliar concepts**](https://sanand0.github.io/talks/2026-03-18-iitm-academic-council/ideas/table.html?id=ai-explainers). AI can generate not just text answers but visual, animated, intuitive explanations. That makes concept onboarding faster for both students and new faculty.

- [**Use AI to generate the content**](https://sanand0.github.io/talks/2026-03-18-iitm-academic-council/ideas/table.html?id=ai-comics). I used to write content. Then I linked to the best content online -- it's better than mine. Now, AI drafts comics, interactive explainers, and simulators. My job is to pick good topics and generate in good formats.

nodes.name: Coding Agents as Exam QA Mirrors
nodes.description: Use coding agents to probe an exam before students see it so you can find weak questions early. If an agent solves something instantly, that is a sign to ask what the question is really measuring in a world where AI is available. This is useful as both a quality check and a reality check, but it can only tell you so much about human learning.

source_tags.search_text: - [**Use coding agents to test the exam before students do**](https://sanand0.github.io/talks/2026-03-18-iitm-academic-council/ideas/table.html?id=agent-exam-testing). If an agent solves a question instantly, you should ask what the question is actually measuring. Agents become both QA tools and mirrors for curricular relevance.

nodes.name: Non-Coders Building Interactive Learning Tools
nodes.description: Make it possible for non-coders to build interactive learning tools with AI, like timelines, maps, biographies, and explainers. That widens who can try AI-native teaching, so it is not limited to computer science or people who already know how to code. It works well in fields that need communication and structure, and less well when the tool needs heavy engineering or frequent maintenance.

source_tags.search_text: - [**Let non-coders build interactive learning tools**](https://sanand0.github.io/talks/2026-03-18-iitm-academic-council/ideas/table.html?id=no-code-tools). AI lowers the cost of making timelines, maps, biographies, and interactive explainers. That opens AI-native pedagogy far beyond computer science into humanities and social sciences.

nodes.name: Instrumented Student AI Usage
nodes.description: Once student AI use is instrumented, you can stop guessing and start seeing what learners actually do. Model choice, help-seeking, retries, and timing can show which habits help and which ones get in the way, which makes this useful for redesigning support and pacing. It works best when the data is reliable and used carefully, because telemetry alone does not explain every choice.

source_tags.search_text: - [**Track which AI tools students actually use**](https://sanand0.github.io/talks/2026-03-18-iitm-academic-council/ideas/table.html?id=track-ai-tools). Once AI use is instrumented, you stop guessing. You can see which models students choose, when they ask for help, and what behavior actually correlates with success.

- [**Redesign exams from behavior data, not intuition**](https://sanand0.github.io/talks/2026-03-18-iitm-academic-council/ideas/table.html?id=behavior-data-exam). Model choice, timing, retry patterns, and deadline behavior all reveal how students really work. That should feed back into question design, support strategy, and pacing.

nodes.name: Canonical Q&A Cards for Humans and Bots
nodes.description: Turn repeated questions into canonical Q&A cards so the answer does not live in one person’s head or in some old thread. In practice, this gives both humans and bots one clear thing to cite, which reduces drift and confusion. It works best for questions that keep coming back, and less well for cases that need judgment, context, or a fresh answer.

source_tags.search_text: - [**Turn recurring answers into canonical Q&A cards**](https://sanand0.github.io/talks/2026-03-18-iitm-academic-council/ideas/table.html?id=canonical-qa). Once the same question appears three times, it should stop living in somebody's head or an old thread. Convert it into a canonical artifact that both humans and bots can cite consistently.

nodes.name: Closed-Book Solo Problem Solving
nodes.description: Treat real-world problem solving as something more than a closed-book, solo act. That stance blocks the kind of AI-native collaboration and tool use students will actually need outside the classroom. It fails because it pushes practice away from how work is done in real settings, where asking for help and using resources is normal.

source_tags.search_text: Stop pretending that real-world problem solving is closed-book and solo.

nodes.name: Blanket Anti-Copying Rules
nodes.description: Blanket anti-copying rules treat all similarity as if it were the same problem. That misses the difference between cheating, imitation, and the normal act of learning from others, especially when AI makes reuse even easier to detect poorly. This approach works badly when you want originality without punishing collaboration, because it hides the real question of what kind of originality you want.

source_tags.search_text: Blanket anti-copying rules assume that all similarity is bad.

nodes.name: Identical Problems With Shared Answer Paths
nodes.description: Giving everyone the same problem with the same visible answer path makes answer-sharing the easiest strategy. In practice, that shifts attention away from solving and toward finding or passing around the answer. It fails whenever the task is predictable enough to leak, which is why unique variants matter if you want students to actually work the problem.

source_tags.search_text: If everyone sees the same problem with the same visible answer path, answer-sharing becomes the dominant strategy.

nodes.name: Final-Artifact-Only Authenticity Checks
nodes.description: When outputs can be copied or AI-generated, the final submission by itself does not tell you much. The process trace matters more, because it shows whether the student actually knew how to do the work. Logs, notes, recordings, and agent traces help reveal real ownership, especially when the polished result could have been faked or heavily assisted.

source_tags.search_text: When outputs can be copied or AI-generated, the trace becomes more valuable than the final artifact.

nodes.name: String-Matching Plagiarism Detection
nodes.description: Simple string matching breaks down quickly in AI-era plagiarism checks. It misses cases where students rename variables or reformat code, while still looking for the wrong kind of similarity. Structural methods like tokenizing, MinHash, and clustering work better because they catch meaningful overlap and can separate real collaboration from suspicious copying.

source_tags.search_text: Use structural similarity, not string matching

nodes.name: Syntax-and-Routine Curriculum Lock-In
nodes.description: A curriculum that stays centered on syntax and routine execution keeps attention on the parts of work AI is already weakening. That can leave students less prepared for the harder tasks that matter more now, like judgment, debugging, integration, validation, orchestration, and taste. In practice, this means teaching upward toward skills that still need human thinking instead of freezing the course at the old baseline.

source_tags.search_text: The curriculum should move upward, not cling to the parts AI is already eating.

nodes.name: Smooth-Progression-Only Learning Design
nodes.description: If learning is always smooth and tidy, students do not get much practice with the kind of problems they will face in real work. Messy settings are often partly wrong, incomplete, hidden, or a little adversarial, and that is where people learn to adapt. A polished path can feel better in class, but it does not build the same resilience or problem-solving range.

source_tags.search_text: Smooth progression alone doesn't build that.

nodes.name: Lecture-Only AI Skills Instruction
nodes.description: Talking about embeddings, vision, structured outputs, or hallucinations is not the same as using them well. Students need live tasks with real APIs, time pressure, and uncertainty so they can see how these tools behave when things do not go as planned. That approach works better for building practical skill, while lectures alone tend to stay abstract.

source_tags.search_text: Don't just lecture about embeddings, vision, structured outputs, or hallucinations.

nodes.name: One-Line-Answer-Centric Assessment
nodes.description: Assessment systems that only reward neat one-line answers push education toward fake simplicity. Real work is often messy and multimodal, with images, stories, APIs, analyses, and dashboards all mixed together. If the grading system cannot handle that, students are nudged to produce smaller and cleaner answers than the task really calls for.

source_tags.search_text: If assessment automation cannot handle those, it will keep pushing education toward fake neatness.

nodes.name: Correctness-Only Creativity Grading
nodes.description: When courses praise creativity but score only correctness, originality becomes a side note instead of part of the work itself. Students quickly learn that safe, standard answers are what really count. If originality is to matter, it has to be rewarded in the grading structure, not just mentioned in the assignment text.

source_tags.search_text: Most courses praise creativity but grade only correctness.

nodes.name: Personal-Subscription AI Access in Courses
nodes.description: If students need personal subscriptions to use AI in class, then access to the tool is tied to money rather than learning. That quietly makes course outcomes depend on what a student can afford, not just what they can do. Shared, governed access keeps AI as part of the course itself, so it does not become a private advantage.

source_tags.search_text: If AI access depends on personal subscriptions, the institution is quietly grading wealth, not skill.

nodes.name: Raw Logs as Governance Communication
nodes.description: Raw logs are useful evidence, but they do not usually move policy by themselves. Decision-makers need the data turned into narrated replays and plain summaries they can actually read and act on. The point is not just to collect traces, but to make patterns visible in a form that faculty and leaders can use.

source_tags.search_text: Raw logs do not change policy.

nodes.name: Weak-Student Labels Without Failure-Mode Breakdown
nodes.description: Saying someone is a weak student does not tell you what to fix. The useful step is to break the problem into specific failure modes, like misreading the givens, choosing a bad strategy, missing a trap, or not knowing when to stop. That makes improvement possible because each of those failures can be taught and coached separately.

source_tags.search_text: "Weak student" is too vague to be useful.

nodes.name: Data-Viz Style-Guide Fidelity via LLMs
nodes.description: LLMs can often pick up the look of a chart style from examples, but turning that into code that truly follows a real style guide is still extremely hard. In practice, this works better for rough imitation than for faithful application in tools like matplotlib, so it is not something to rely on when consistency matters.

source_tags.search_text: applying them via matplotlib is almost #impossible today.

nodes.name: AI Sketch Notes as Production Output
nodes.description: LLMs still do not make good sketch notes in a way that is ready for production use. They may produce something that looks like sketch notes at a glance, but it still falls short of the quality people expect from a skilled human. That makes it weak as a real output format, not just a rough draft.

source_tags.search_text: - LLMs can't yet create good sketch notes.

nodes.name: Generic LLMs for Music Emotion Reading
nodes.description: Generic multimodal LLMs can transcribe music well enough, but that does not mean they can really hear what the music is doing emotionally. Past transcription, they are mostly guessing about the feeling or intent behind the sound. So they may be useful for basic text extraction, but not for trustworthy music emotion reading.

source_tags.search_text: This confirms a suspicion I had: Gemini can't actually hear the audio. It can transcribe, but beyond that it's just guessing.

nodes.name: Advanced Voice Mode for Long-Pause Dialogue
nodes.description: Voice modes that answer as soon as you pause do not fit conversations where silence is part of the flow. They can work for fast back-and-forth exchanges, but they break down in panel-style talk or reflective dialogue, where you need more control over when the system speaks. If pauses matter, this behavior gets in the way.

source_tags.search_text: But if you pause, it replies immediately. Muting caused the app to hang.

nodes.name: Face-Faithful GPT Image Editing
nodes.description: Even strong image models can still be bad when the main goal is to keep a face looking like the same person. They may produce attractive images overall, but the face fidelity is not dependable enough for work where identity has to stay intact. That makes them useful for some edits, but not for cases where the face is the hard requirement.

source_tags.search_text: But it's still terrible at faces.

nodes.name: Gemini Flash Image for Text-Heavy Renders
nodes.description: Gemini Flash Image still struggles with generating clean text directly inside images. A workaround is to give it the text already rendered as an image, which can help in text-heavy renders. Without that kind of trick, though, it is not a reliable choice when text has to come out clearly.

source_tags.search_text: Gemini 2.5 Flash Image is not great at generating text.

nodes.name: Gemini Flash Image for Direct Style-Transfer Prompts
nodes.description: Gemini Flash Image does not seem to respond well to direct style-transfer prompts. Asking it to turn something into a specific art style can be ignored or only partly followed, so the prompt itself is not a dependable control surface. In practice, that means you should not count on it for precise style transfer.

source_tags.search_text: Gemini 2.5 Flash Image seems to ignore commands that try style transfer (e.g. "turn me into Studio Ghibli").

nodes.name: Thinking-Mode Image Editing for Source Fidelity
nodes.description: When editing images, more reasoning is not always better. In this case, the simpler non-thinking path can preserve the original image features much better than the thinking mode, which may change the person or scene more than you want. That matters when source fidelity is the goal, and it fails when overediting is a risk.

source_tags.search_text: ChatGPT's non-thinking mode does a _much_ better job of preserving the original image features than the thinking mode.

nodes.name: Gemini as Default Noisy-Mobile STT
nodes.description: For everyday Android dictation in noisy conditions, Gemini still has trouble hearing correctly and sometimes stops too early. It may work in cleaner settings, but in real use it can miss words and fail to wait for the end of a thought. That makes it a weak default for mobile speech to text when the environment is not quiet.

source_tags.search_text: Gemini mis-hears and doesn't wait till I'm done.

nodes.name: One-Go Mid-Sized App Generation
nodes.description: Mid-sized apps are still not something you can expect to generate correctly in one pass. The practical mode is still scaffold and refine: get a first version, then fix and extend it step by step. That works for moving faster, but it does not replace the need for iteration on anything beyond the simplest app.

source_tags.search_text: **My learning**: Cursor and I still can’t build a mid-sized app in one go. I’m in the "scaffold and refine" phase.

nodes.name: Big-Upfront SPEC.md Before Seeing the Product
nodes.description: Writing a spec upfront can be useful, especially if parts of it will be reused. But if you lock it in before you have seen the product take shape, you may be planning for a problem that changes during the build. In practice, this works better when the spec is allowed to evolve as you learn what you actually want.

source_tags.search_text: **My takeaway**: Writing a `SPEC.md` can help, especially if you plan to re-use parts of it. But the spec kept evolving as I built the app, because I often don’t know what I want until I see it. Maybe an “agile” approach would work better for me.

nodes.name: Framework-Semantics Guessing Without Runtime Verification
nodes.description: If you let the model guess how a framework works, it can borrow rules from the wrong place and produce code that does not fit the stack. That is how you end up with React-style hooks in code that is not React. Runtime checks matter here, because the mistake may look plausible until you try to run it.

source_tags.search_text: This code was a mess. I had told it to use `lit-html`, which doesn’t have `useState`. That’s a React feature, so nothing worked right.

nodes.name: Human Gut Feel as LLM Eval
nodes.description: Your own sense of whether an LLM output is good can be very easy to fool. When the result matters, you need written evals instead of just trusting a quick gut check. They give you something concrete to compare and make it harder to fool yourself with one nice-looking answer.

source_tags.search_text: I **cannot** trust my judgement. I need written evals. Badly.

nodes.name: Agent Coding Without Consistency Rules
nodes.description: Coding agents often drift across small but important project conventions. They may switch between APIs, change timeouts without reason, or skip fixes that should have stayed consistent. That is a problem when a project needs one clear style and predictable behavior across files.

source_tags.search_text: It flips between `execCommand("copy")` and `clipboard.writeText()`.

nodes.name: Agent Coding Without Edge-Case Discovery
nodes.description: Agents still miss important edge cases unless you make them explicit. A change that looks harmless can break a form submit, require a module script, or fail in some other small but costly way. Tests and adversarial cases help surface those gaps before the code ships.

source_tags.search_text: It missed that. It also left scripts as plain `<script>` instead of `<script type="module">` which was required.

nodes.name: Agent Coding Without Experimental Debugging
nodes.description: Some coding problems are not solved by editing alone. They need small experiments that can prove or disprove a guess, like checking whether a directory is actually served or whether a runtime supports a certain export pattern. Agents often skip that step, which makes them weaker in debugging work.

source_tags.search_text: Coding agents didn't run such experiments.

nodes.name: Iterating on Directionally Wrong Agent Solutions
nodes.description: Once a model starts from the wrong basic idea, more iterations often just refine the wrong plan. That can make the output look better without making it correct. In those cases, starting over is usually more useful than polishing what is already off track.

source_tags.search_text: if a solution is directionally wrong, restarting works better than iterating.

nodes.name: Heavy Opinionated AI Platforms and Proxies
nodes.description: Heavy AI platforms and proxies add another layer right when the underlying vendor APIs are changing quickly. That extra abstraction can become brittle and make updates harder to absorb. Thin API-level layers are safer when you want the stack to stay flexible and not break as the vendors move.

source_tags.search_text: Avoid heavy, opinionated AI platforms and proxies (like EPAM DIAL) in favor of thin API-level layers

nodes.name: Vague AI Initiatives
nodes.description: Big AI initiative language can sound active while staying too broad to guide real work. If the goal is not narrowed into a specific, testable bet, teams can spend a lot of time talking without learning much. It works better when the intended output is clear from the start, whether that is a tool, a plan, a feasibility check, or a pilot.

source_tags.search_text: Broad and vague AI initiatives are likely to fail; they must be narrowed into specific, testable bets to be effective.

nodes.name: Constraint-Blind AI Transformation
nodes.description: AI transformation plans fail when they ignore the things most likely to break: people and habits, existing tools and infrastructure, and client expectations. Those limits are not side issues; they shape what can actually change. Real gains come from redesigning the workflow around the new technology, not from dropping AI into one old step.

source_tags.search_text: AI transformation is limited by three "breakage" points that must be managed: legacy people/habits, existing tooling/infrastructure, and client expectations.

nodes.name: Top-K RAG for Exhaustive Guideline Proofing
nodes.description: Standard top-K RAG is not the right shape when the task is exhaustive proofing against a strict set of guidelines. It only retrieves the most relevant matches, so it can miss needed evidence and leave gaps. In practice, this matters when you need full coverage, not just a good answer. It works better for search and recall than for proving that every guideline has been checked.

source_tags.search_text: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is inherently non-exhaustive because it only retrieves "top-K" matches

nodes.name: Zero-Leak PII Guarantees
nodes.description: A promise of perfect zero-leak PII protection is not credible with current AI models. Even high accuracy can still leave a large number of misses when the volume is big, so the risk does not go away in production. This matters anywhere sensitive images or documents are filtered at scale. The idea can support careful reduction of leaks, but not a true guarantee.

source_tags.search_text: Accept that a 100% "zero leak" guarantee for PII is technologically impossible with current AI models

nodes.name: Current Computer-Use Tools for Routine Desktop Automation
nodes.description: Current computer-use tools for desktop automation are still too slow and too expensive for many normal-budget workflows. That makes them a poor default when the same job can be done with cheaper web automation or a simpler manual step. They may still be useful for high-budget or hard-to-reach desktop tasks. For routine work, though, they often add cost without enough payoff.

source_tags.search_text: Avoid automating desktop applications unless the budget is very high, as current "Computer Use" AI tools are still relatively slow and expensive.

nodes.name: Model-Coupled Business Logic
nodes.description: If business logic is hard-coded into one specific model’s behavior, you build up AI debt. That makes it harder and more expensive to switch later when a better or cheaper model appears. In practice, this locks your system to one model’s quirks instead of keeping the logic portable. It works against long-term flexibility and raises the cost of change.

source_tags.search_text: hard-coding business logic into specific models creates "AI debt," making it expensive and difficult to switch to better or cheaper models later.

nodes.name: Model Upgrades as Drop-In Swaps
nodes.description: Treating a model upgrade like a simple drop-in swap is risky. A smarter model may read prompts differently, which can change outputs and break downstream parsers or other code that depended on the old behavior. This matters most in systems with structured outputs and tight integrations. The safer view is to treat upgrades as migrations, with testing and cleanup.

source_tags.search_text: Treat model upgrades as migrations rather than simple swaps

nodes.name: Template-Free Multimodal Layout Automation
nodes.description: Current LLMs are not ready to do fully automated spatial and layout work without strong templates and constraints. They can help with pieces of the job, but they still struggle with the kind of visual coordination that makes a slide or page hold together. This matters when the layout has to be exact. Without guardrails, the system can miss what changed in one area while breaking another.

source_tags.file: Dropbox/notes/transcripts/extract/ai/2026-03-03 WK Wolters Kluwer Verdict to Video.md
source_tags.search_text: making 100% automation without strict templates unrealistic in the short term.

nodes.name: AI as Iterative Slide Micro-Editor
nodes.description: Using AI to make tiny slide edits is still a trap. A small nudge can disturb other parts of the slide that were already correct, so the edit cost can spread beyond the intended change. This matters most when you are trying to polish a near-finished layout instead of generating one from scratch. It works better with rigid templates than with free-form iterative tweaking.

source_tags.file: Dropbox/notes/transcripts/extract/ai/2026-03-03 WK Wolters Kluwer Verdict to Video.md
source_tags.search_text: Do not use AI as an iterative slide editor, as asking it to make minor adjustments often causes it to "break" the other 75% of the slide that was previously correct.

nodes.name: Pinpoint for Investigations and Audit Trails
nodes.description: Pinpoint looks useful for digging through large collections of documents, audio, and images when you need traces for an investigation. It can help surface mentions, people, places, and links across messy material, which is valuable for audit trails and internal review. At the same time, it reads more like a watchlist and exploration surface than a fully committed workflow. It is promising for analysis, but not yet something to bet the whole process on.

source_tags.search_text: Use it for _internal investigations / audit trails_

nodes.name: Project Mariner for Supervised Workflow Prototyping
nodes.description: Project Mariner looks like a good watchlist item for supervised browser-task prototyping. It is meant to take actions on your behalf while a person stays in the loop, which makes it useful for testing a real workflow before you invest in full automation. That can save time when you want to see whether a browser job is worth building properly. It is more of a prototype surface than a finished automation stack.

source_tags.search_text: is a Google Labs "agentic" prototype aimed at taking actions on your behalf in a supervised way.

nodes.name: Firebase Studio as Browser-Native Agentic Workspace
nodes.description: Firebase Studio is worth evaluating as a browser-first workspace for building full-stack apps with agents. The appeal is that you can work on internal tools without local setup, then move toward deployment through Firebase or Cloud Run. That makes it practical for fast prototyping of real products, especially when the team wants less environment overhead. It is strongest for no-local-setup flows and internal tool builds.

source_tags.search_text: is a browser-based "full-stack AI workspace" with agents, unifying Project IDX into Firebase.

nodes.name: LIT for Interactive Model Probing
nodes.description: LIT is an interactive place to inspect how a model behaves across text, image, or tabular inputs. In practice, it helps you compare controlled changes like tense, style, or sensitive attributes and see what shifts in the output. That makes it useful when you need to debug brittleness or understand what the model is actually using, but it works best when you already have a specific behavior to probe.

source_tags.search_text: is an interactive UI for probing models on text/image/tabular data.

nodes.name: What-If Tool for Counterfactual Fairness Checks
nodes.description: The What-If Tool is a low-code way to test model predictions and fairness by making small counterfactual edits. You can take one example, change it in a few ways, and see which feature flips the decision. That makes it handy for finding weak spots and deciding what data to collect next, especially when you want a quick check without writing much code.

source_tags.search_text: is a minimal-coding tool to probe model predictions and fairness.

nodes.name: Facets for Dataset Skew and Leakage Audits
nodes.description: Facets helps you explore a dataset visually so you can spot skew, outliers, missingness, and leakage before they become bigger problems. In practice, it is useful for checking whether groups are unevenly represented or whether the training data has odd patterns that will mislead a model. It is strongest early on, when a data fix is better than trying to tune around a bad dataset.

source_tags.search_text: helps you explore and visualize datasets to catch skew, outliers, and leakage early.

nodes.name: Antigravity as Multi-Agent Dev Environment Watchlist
nodes.description: Antigravity is a development environment to watch if you want multiple agents working on different tasks at once. The basic idea is that one agent can debug while another refactors or writes tests, with you keeping an eye on the results. It matters most when parallel work could save time, but it still reads as something to evaluate rather than a tool to rely on fully.

source_tags.search_text: is positioned as an agentic development environment.

nodes.name: Opal for Natural-Language Mini-App Workflows
nodes.description: Opal is a way to build, edit, and share small AI apps from natural language, with a workflow editor behind it. In practice, that makes it good for repeatable analyst tasks like pulling recent news, summarizing risks, and drafting outreach from a company name. It works best when the task is structured and repeated often, not when you need a one-off creative burst.

source_tags.search_text: builds, edits, and shares AI mini-apps from natural language (with a workflow editor).

nodes.name: Mixboard for Structured Ideation Sprints
nodes.description: Mixboard is an AI concepting board for exploring and refining ideas. You can use it to generate a lot of variants, group the ones that belong together, and then turn the best few into clean summaries or one-pagers. It fits structured ideation well, but it is less about final production and more about helping you sort through rough options.

source_tags.search_text: is an AI concepting canvas/board for exploring and refining ideas.

nodes.name: Stitch for Prompt/Sketch-to-UI Drafting
nodes.description: Stitch turns prompts or sketches into UI designs and code. In practice, it is useful when you want to go from a rough wireframe to starter React or Tailwind code the same day. It is a good fit for early drafts and handoff pieces, but not a substitute for finished design work.

source_tags.search_text: turns prompts/sketches into UI designs and code.

nodes.name: Model Garden for Governed Model Shootouts
nodes.description: Model Garden is Vertex AI’s catalog for finding, testing, customizing, and deploying models from Google and partners. That makes it useful for a controlled model comparison flow, where you run the same eval set on a few candidates and then pick the winner behind one governed platform. It matters most in enterprise settings where standard controls and repeatable testing are part of the job.

source_tags.search_text: - 🟡 [Model Garden](https://cloud.google.com/model-garden) is Vertex AI’s catalog to discover, test, customize, and deploy models from Google and partners. E.g. shortlist 3 candidate models, run the same eval set, then deploy the winner behind one standardized platform with enterprise controls

nodes.name: Google AI Edge for On-Device AI
nodes.description: Google AI Edge is the umbrella for building AI that runs on device across mobile and web. In practice, it is the kind of stack you use when offline use, privacy, or low latency matters more than depending on the network. It is a strong fit for on-device features, but it only helps when local deployment is actually the right tradeoff.

source_tags.search_text: is the umbrella for building on-device AI (mobile/web) with ready-to-use APIs across vision, audio, text, and genAI.

nodes.name: Teachable Machine for Rapid Browser ML
nodes.description: Teachable Machine lets people train simple image, sound, or pose models right in the browser and export them. That makes it a fast way to prototype a small custom classifier or demo, like a gesture or sound trigger for accessibility. It is useful when speed and ease matter most, but it is meant for lightweight models rather than serious production training.

source_tags.search_text: lets anyone train simple image/sound/pose models in the browser and export them.
